revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Hori 10th Anniversary Tekken 5 Arcade Stick | Guid=3000-140 | Deck=A limited edition arcade stick released as part of the Ultimate Collector's Edition of Tekken 5 for PlayStation 2. Only 20,000 were made. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395935-4637497106-s-l50.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395935-4637497106-s-l50.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_super,scale_small,scale_medium,square_medium,square_mini,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A limited edition arcade stick released as part of the Ultimate Collector's Edition of Tekken 5 for PlayStation 2. Only 20,000 were made.

{{Accessory | Name=Hori Dragon Quest Slime Controller | Guid=3000-139 | Deck=Originally released for the PlayStation 2 to coincide with Dragon Quest VIII in 2005, Hori's slime controller has seen several iterations for other consoles since then. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395904-0621993891-s-l64.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395904-0621993891-s-l64.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,scale_medium,scale_small,square_small,square_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Originally released for the PlayStation 2 to coincide with Dragon Quest VIII in 2005, Hori's slime controller has seen several iterations for other consoles since then.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-32)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Hori Real Arcade Pro 3 | Guid=3000-32 | Deck=The third in Hori's Real Arcade Pro line is a PlayStation 3 joystick, primarily for use with fighting games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/56/678879-horirealarcadepro3.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"678879-horirealarcadepro3.jpg","path":"0\/56\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_avatar,square_small,scale_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini,scale_tiny,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":"Hori Real Arcade Pro 3"},"background":[]}' />
==Parts==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_medium/12/121177/1680296-389352816_2e7de520a8.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/12/121177/1680296-389352816_2e7de520a8.jpg|align=right|caption=HRAP, colored wires, and square gate. }} The Hori Real Arcade Pro list of parts :
* [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanwa_Denshi Sanwa]'' ''JLF-TP8Y-SK Joystick''. '' * 35 mm Ball top. * 30 mm snap in buttons, with color coded wires and quick disconnects. * 24 mm snap in start and select buttons. * Traditional Japanese square gate * Wired USB connection, 3 meters long
==Different Versions of the HRAP3== '' {{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_small/12/121177/1707512-hrap_3sa_w.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/12/121177/1707512-hrap_3sa_w.jpg|align=right|caption=Hori Real Arcade Pro 3 SA}} '' *please only post versions the HRAP3, not other HRAP models
A special edition of the Hori Real Arcade Pro was released called the Hori Real Arcade Pro Special Edition. Aesthetically the HRAP3 SA differs from the standard version; It features all white buttons ,and a brushed stainless faceplate. The SA uses OBSF-30/24 buttons and the same JLF-TP8Y-SK joystick as the standard version.
==Modifying ==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_small/12/121177/1680299-2838492277_85fd7855e1_o_710x475.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/12/121177/1680299-2838492277_85fd7855e1_o_710x475.jpg|align=right|caption=Modded HRAP with new buttons and "bubble" ball top }} The Hori Real Arcade Pro 3 uses standard snap in 30 mm arcade buttons that are wired with quick disconnects. Most arcade sticks that where released before the HRAP have had there buttons soldered directly to the PCB. This makes the Hori Real Arcade Pro vary easy to modify, because no soldering equipment or skills are required.

page.title: Accessories/Hori Tekken 6 Limited Edition Wireless Fight Stick

revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Hori Tekken 6 Limited Edition Wireless Fight Stick | Guid=3000-175 | Deck=The Hori Tekken 6 Wireless Fight Stick was part of a limited edition bundle with the game in October 2009. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396656-8924903791-tekke.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396656-8924903791-tekke.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_small,square_medium,scale_super,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,scale_large,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Hori Tekken 6 Wireless Fight Stick was part of a limited edition bundle with the game in October 2009.

{{Accessory | Name=ION Drum Rocker | Guid=3000-27 | Deck=The ION Drum Rocker was designed to provide a more authentic drumming experience, with the Rock Band platform in mind. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/4/45179/1167979-2662936759_93d810d2cf.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"1167979-2662936759_93d810d2cf.jpg","path":"4\/45179\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_avatar,scale_small,scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,square_small,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":"ION Drum Rocker"},"background":[]}' /> The [http://www.drumrocker.com/products/index.php ION Drum Rocker] is an electronic drum kit that is officially compatible with the [[Games/Rock_Band|Rock Band]], [[Games/Rock_Band_2|Rock Band 2]] and [[Games/Rock_Band_3|Rock Band 3]] games. The kit features an adjustable aluminum frame which holds the pads and cymbals. The pads and cymbals, as well as the kick-pedal, plug into a center module, which itself plugs into the Xbox 360 via USB. The Drum Rocker launched at $299.99, but with a separate add-on module, it becomes a fully-functioning electronic drum set.
After release, a patch was made available to allow owners to use their Drum Rocker with [[Games/Guitar_Hero_World_Tour|Guitar Hero World Tour]]. When using this accessory, the drum track in the game is reduced from 5 triggers plus bass drum, to 4 triggers plus bass drum, to match the available hardware.
The Ion Drum Rocker is available for the [[Platforms/Xbox_360|Xbox 360]],[[Objects/PlayStation_3|PS3]]/[[Objects/PlayStation_2|PS2]], and [[Platforms/Wii|Wii]].

{{Accessory | Name=Intellivoice | Guid=3000-66 | Deck=A voice synthesis expandable add-on for the Intellivision platform. It plugged in between the game cartridge and the system. If a game utilized it, real voices were to be heard from the TV. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/13/132099/2546235-3193082135-Intel.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2546235-3193082135-Intel.jpg","path":"13\/132099\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_mini,scale_small,square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> A voice synthesis expandable add-on for the Intellivision platform. It plugged in between the game cartridge and the system. If a game utilized it, real voices were to be heard from the TV.

{{Accessory | Name=Jabra BT125 Bluetooth Headset | Guid=3000-176 | Deck=The Jabra BT125 Bluetooth Headset was bundled with copies of Warhawk on PS3 to facilitate online play. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396662-1326325923-s-l60.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396662-1326325923-s-l60.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_medium,scale_medium,scale_small,scale_super,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Jabra BT125 Bluetooth Headset was bundled with copies of Warhawk on PS3 to facilitate online play.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-34)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Jogcon | Guid=3000-34 | Deck=The Jogcon is a controller developed and produced by Namco for their Ridge Racer 4 videogame. It features a unique steering wheel device in the center of the controller. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/56/678871-jogcon.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"678871-jogcon.png","path":"0\/56\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"square_avatar,square_small,scale_small,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' />
==Overview==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_small/4/47864/1358597-aaa.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/4/47864/1358597-aaa.jpg|align=right|caption=The Jogcon featuring the wheel device on the center.}} The Jogcon was designed to combine the functionality of a real steering wheel with the size of a standard PlayStation DualShock Controller. This is accomplished by a small wheel device located at the center of the peripheral.
Originally released in 1998 as part of a special edition package with [[Companies/Bandai_Namco_Entertainment|Namco]]'s [[Games/R4_Ridge_Racer_Type_4|Ridge Racer Type 4]] game, it was unique in that it provided accurate force feedback with every torque steer and collision that occurred in the game.
The Jogcon would later be sold separately from the Ridge Racer game.
==Naming== The name was derived from the actions of 'jogging' a dial on a VCR.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-101)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Joy-Con | Guid=3000-101 | Deck=Joy-Con is the primary input device for the Nintendo Switch. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/16/164924/2935953-joycon.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2935953-joycon.png","path":"16\/164924\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"square_medium,square_small,scale_medium,scale_super,scale_small,square_tiny,scale_tiny,square_mini,ignore_jpg_scale_medium,ignore_jpg_scale_small,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' />
The Nintendo Switch ships with two Joy-Con, along with a wrist strap for each. They can be used and charged while attached to the Nintendo Switch console via rails, or they can be used while detached. Detaching the Joy-Con allows them to be used in other accessories, or they can be used for two-player.
==Features== Each Joy-Con has an analog stick, four digital direction buttons, a - or + button, and three 'shoulder' buttons, one on top of the controller and two more on the rail. The left Joy-Con has a capture button, while the right Joy-Con has a Home button.
Joy-Con have HD Rumble, which allows them to vibrate to create precise sensations, such as balls rolling in a box.
{{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/16/164924/2935953-joycon.png|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/16/164924/2935953-joycon.png|align=right|width=320}} Each Joy-Con have an accelerometer and gyroscope that can be used for motion tracking. They can allow pointing controls such as the Wii and Wii U had without the need for a sensor bar.
The right Joy-Con has an infrared depth tracking sensor capable of distinguishing objects and shapes. It also has an NFC reader to use for Amiibo.
The batteries have a reported life of 20 hours.

{{Accessory | Name=Joy-Con 2 | Guid=3000-200 | Deck=Joy-Con 2 is the standard controller for Nintendo Switch 2. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3717030-1772627598-Frame_3473721.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3717030-1772627598-Frame_3473721.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_small,square_medium,scale_medium,scale_super,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,square_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Joy-Con 2 is the standard controller for Nintendo Switch 2.

{{Accessory | Name=Joy-Con 2 Charging Grip | Guid=3000-201 | Deck=A controller attachment that holds two Joy-Con 2 controllers to give the feel of a classic controller while also giving a convenient charging option. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3717034-6964895315-Frame_3473716.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3717034-6964895315-Frame_3473716.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,scale_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A controller attachment that holds two Joy-Con 2 controllers to give the feel of a classic controller while also giving a convenient charging option.

{{Accessory | Name=Joy-Con 2 Wheel | Guid=3000-202 | Deck=A steering wheel controller attachment for Nintendo Switch 2. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3717038-7141013224-Frame_3473690.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3717038-7141013224-Frame_3473690.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,scale_super,square_tiny,square_small,scale_medium,scale_small,square_mini,scale_large,screen_medium,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A steering wheel controller attachment for Nintendo Switch 2.

{{Accessory | Name=Joyboard | Guid=3000-68 | Deck=The first commercially available balance board controller for a video game console | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/4647/2601741-4745262119-Joybo.JPG}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2601741-4745262119-Joybo.JPG","path":"0\/4647\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_mini,square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_tiny,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> The Joyboard is a balance board controller developed by [[Companies/Amiga_Corporation|Amiga Corporation]] for the Atari 2600. A player stands on the board and leans in any direction, mimicking the action of a joystick. A port on the Joyboard allows a regular joystick to plug through, so the Joyboard doesn't need to be removed from the console when not in use. Amiga released a single title, [[Games/Mogul_Maniac|Mogul Maniac]], for use with the Joyboard, but [[Games/Off_Your_Rocker|Off Your Rocker]] was developed for the controller, and sold through a third party. A third title, Surf's Up, was developed but never released. The legacy of Joyboard includes other balance board controllers, such as the Roll n Rocker and the [[Accessories/Wii_Balance_Board|Wii Balance Board.]]

{{Accessory | Name=Kinect for Xbox 360 | Guid=3000-35 | Deck=Kinect for Xbox 360 is a set of cameras and sensors designed for hands-free gaming. It was released in North America on November 4th, 2010. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/8272/1344510-project_natal2.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"1344510-project_natal2.jpg","path":"0\/8272\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_avatar,square_small,scale_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini,scale_tiny,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' />
==Device== Kinect is a full-body motion tracker peripheral available for the [[Platforms/Xbox_360|Microsoft Xbox 360]]. The unit consists of a motorized sensor bar that is approximately 9 inches in length that is affixed in front of or on top of the screen to track players via three cameras. One is infrared to determine depth and two additional cameras to see in 3 dimensions. The most advanced aspect of the device is the software that converts the data captured by the cameras into game input. There was some controversy in Microsoft's decision during development to move more processing from the Kinect device itself to the Xbox 360 host system in an apparent move to keep the cost of the device down. The Kinect unit is able to detect 48 joints at 30 frames per second. The device is capable to adapt to any controller even that of the [[Concepts/PlayStation_Move_Support|PlayStation Move]]. This can allow the developer to easily create a wireless low-powered motion controller specific to their game. [[Accessories/Kinect_for_Xbox_One|A short-lived successor]] to the original Kinect was developed for the [[Platforms/Xbox_One|Xbox One]].
==Public Appearances Pre-Launch== Kinect has made several noteworthy appearances. It was first announced and demonstrated to the public at [[Concepts/E3|E3]], June 1, 2009. It has also been demonstrated on [http://www.gametrailers.com/user-movie/jimmy-fallon-project-natal/320589 Late Night with Jimmy Fallon] on June 11, 2009. It was seen in the Smallville episode '[http://www.cwtv.com/cw-video/smallville Warrior]' on February 12, 2010.

{{Accessory | Name=Kinect for Xbox One | Guid=3000-183 | Deck=The second generation Kinect sensor bar was originally included with every Xbox One console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396733-0285169523-51IYA.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396733-0285169523-51IYA.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_small,scale_super,scale_medium,square_mini,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Some controversy was raised when it was announced that the new Kinect would be included and be a mandatory device for any Xbox One to reach full utility. After this announcement did not go well over with fans, the Xbox team backpedaled and eventually dropped the mandate from all future Xbox consoles.

{{Accessory | Name=Leap Motion Controller | Guid=3000-60 | Deck=A device for PC and Mac that uses motion controls to interact with apps and games designed for it. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/11/115767/2519417-what1_desktop-45f327da2a167ff91c6eb5392ff9e969.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2519417-what1_desktop-45f327da2a167ff91c6eb5392ff9e969.jpg","path":"11\/115767\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,square_mini,scale_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_tiny,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> A device for PC and Mac that uses motion controls to interact with apps and games designed for it.

{{Accessory | Name=Logitech Cordless Action Controller | Guid=3000-141 | Deck=A third-party controller for the PlayStation 2 and considered by many to be the best wireless option for the console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395942-1055565161-41W3N.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395942-1055565161-41W3N.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_medium,scale_super,scale_small,scale_medium,square_tiny,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_mini,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A third-party controller for the PlayStation 2 and considered by many to be the best wireless option for the console.

{{Accessory | Name=Logitech Cordless Precision Controller | Guid=3000-150 | Deck=A third-party controller for the Xbox and considered by many to be the best wireless option for the console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396059-7359342967-13043.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396059-7359342967-13043.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,square_small,scale_small,square_tiny,scale_medium,scale_super,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A third-party controller for the Xbox and considered by many to be the best wireless option for the console.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-64)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Logitech Driving Force GT | Guid=3000-64 | Deck=A Gran Turismo-branded racing wheel, compatible with PS2, PS3, and PC. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/5/59593/2605867-2533807-5813242949-drivi.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2605867-2533807-5813242949-drivi.png","path":"5\/59593\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"square_tiny,square_medium,scale_small,square_mini,square_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_tiny,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' />
==Overview==
==Features==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_small/21/218838/2572061-logitech+driving+force+gt.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/21/218838/2572061-logitech+driving+force+gt.jpg|align=right}} * Force feedback * 900-degree steering * Real-time adjustment dial (only works with GT5P, GT5 and GT6) * Gas and brake pedals * Sequential stick shift * Paddle shifters
==PC Requirements== * Windows 8, 7, Vista or XP * 20 MB of available hard disk space * CD-ROM drive * Available USB port
==Game Compatibility==
====PS3==== * [[Games/Burnout_Paradise|Burnout Paradise]] * [[Games/DiRT|Dirt]] * [[Games/DiRT_2|Dirt 2]] * [[Games/DiRT_3|Dirt 3]] * [[Games/F1_2010|F1 2010]] * [[Games/F1_2011|F1 2011]] * [[Games/F1_2012|F1 2012]] * [[Games/Ferrari_Challenge_Trofeo_Pirelli|Ferrari Challenge Trofeo Pirelli]] * [[Games/Formula_One_Championship_Edition|Formula One Championship Edition]] * [[Games/Grand_Theft_Auto_IV|Grand Theft Auto IV]] * [[Games/Gran_Turismo_HD|Gran Turismo HD]] * [[Games/Gran_Turismo_5_Prologue|Gran Turismo 5 Prologue]] * [[Games/Gran_Turismo_5|Gran Turismo 5]] * [[Games/Gran_Turismo_6|Gran Turismo 6]] * [[Games/Initial_D_Extreme_Stage|Initial D Extreme Stage]] * [[Games/Juiced_2_Hot_Import_Nights|Juiced 2: Hot Import Nights]] * [[Games/Midnight_Club_Los_Angeles|Midnight Club: Los Angeles]] * [[Games/MotorStorm_Apocalypse|MotorStorm: Apocalypse]] * [[Games/NASCAR_08|NASCAR 08]] * [[Games/NASCAR_09|NASCAR 09]] * [[Games/NASCAR_2011_The_Game|NASCAR 2011: The Game]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_Carbon|Need for Speed: Carbon]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_Hot_Pursuit|Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_ProStreet|Need for Speed: ProStreet]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_The_Run|Need for Speed: The Run]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_Shift|Need for Speed: SHIFT]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_Undercover|Need for Speed: Undercover]] * [[Games/Need_for_Speed_Most_Wanted_2012|Need for Speed: Most Wanted (2012)]] * [[Games/GRID|GRID]] * [[Games/Sega_Rally_Revo|Sega Rally Revo]] * [[Games/Shift_2_Unleashed|SHIFT 2: Unleashed]] * [[Games/Sonic_And_Sega_All_Stars_Racing|Sonic andamp;amp;amp; Sega All-Stars Racing]] * [[Games/Stuntman_Ignition|Stuntman: Ignition]] * [[Games/Supercar_Challenge|Supercar Challenge]] * [[Games/Superstars_V8_Racing|Superstars V8 Racing]] * [[Games/Test_Drive_Unlimited_2|Test Drive Unlimited 2]] * [[Games/WRC_FIA_World_Rally_Championship|WRC: FIA World Rally Championship]] * [[Games/WRC_3|WRC 3]]
====PS2==== * [[Games/007_Nightfire|007: Nightfire]] * [[Games/Eighteen_Wheeler_American_Pro_Trucker|Eighteen Wheeler: American Pro Trucker]] * [[Games/4x4_Evolution|4x4 Evolution]] * [[Games/Auto_Modellista|Auto Modellista]] * [[Games/Burnout|Burnout]] * [[Games/Burnout_2_Point_of_Impact|Burnout 2: Point of Impact]] * [[Games/Burnout_3_Takedown|Burnout 3: Takedown]] * [[Games/Burnout_Revenge|Burnout Revenge]] * [[Games/Burnout_Dominator|Burnout Dominator]] * [[Games/Colin_McRae_Rally_3|Colin McRae Rally 3]] * [[Games/Colin_McRae_Rally_04|Colin McRae Rally 04]] * [[Games/Colin_McRae_Rally_2005|Colin McRae Rally 2005]] * [[Games/Corvette|Corvette]] * [[Games/Corvette_Evolution_GT|Corvette Evolution GT]] * Driven * EA Sports F1 2001 * EA Sports F1 2002 * EA Sports F1 Career Challenge * Enthusia Professional Racing * Ferrari Challenge: Trofeo Pirelli * Ferrari F355 Challenge * FlatOut * FlatOut 2 * Ford Mustang: The Legend Lives * Ford vs. Chevy * Formula One 2001 * Formula One 2002 * Formula One 2003 * Formula One 04 * Formula One 05 * Formula One 06 * Gran Turismo 3: A-Spec * Gran Turismo 4 * Gran Turismo 4 Prologue * Gran Turismo Concept * Grand Prix Challenge * Grand Theft Auto III * Grand Theft Auto: Vice City * Hot Wheels Velocity X * Hot Wheels World Race * Hot Wheels: Stunt Track Challenge * IndyCar Series * IndyCar Series 2005 * Initial D: Special Stage * Juiced (video game) * Juiced 2: Hot Import Nights * Jurassic Park: Operation Genesis * Knight Rider: The Game * Knight Rider 2: The Game * Lotus Challenge * Midnight Club II * Midnight Club 3: DUB Edition * Motor Mayhem * NASCAR 06: Total Team Control * NASCAR 07 * NASCAR 08 * NASCAR 09 * NASCAR: Dirt to Daytona * NASCAR Heat 2002 * NASCAR Thunder 2002 * NASCAR Thunder 2003 * NASCAR Thunder 2004 * NASCAR 2005: Chase for the Cup * Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit 2 * Need for Speed: Underground * Need for Speed: Underground 2 * Need for Speed: Most Wanted * Need for Speed: Carbon * Need for Speed: ProStreet * Need for Speed: Undercover * NHRA Championship Drag Racing * R: Racing Evolution * Rally Championship * Rally Fusion: Race of Champions * Richard Burns Rally * RoadKill * Sega Rally 2006 * Shox * Smuggler's Run 2: Hostile Territory * Starsky & Hutch * The Simpsons: Hit & Run * The Simpsons: Road Rage * Total Immersion Racing * Test Drive Overdrive * Test Drive: Eve of Destruction * TOCA Race Driver 2 * TOCA Race Driver 3 * Tokyo Xtreme Racer 3 * Tokyo Xtreme Racer: Drift 2 * Total Immersion Racing * Twisted Metal: Black * Twisted Metal: Head-On * V-Rally 3 * World of Outlaws: Sprint Cars 2002 * World Rally Championship * WRC II Extreme * WRC 3 * WRC 4 * WRC: Rally Evolved
====PC==== * 18 Wheels of Steel: Across America * 18 Wheels of Steel: American Long Haul * 18 Wheels of Steel: Convoy * 18 Wheels of Steel: Extreme Trucker * 18 Wheels of Steel: Extreme Trucker 2 * 18 Wheels of Steel: Hard Truck * 18 Wheels of Steel: Haulin' * 18 Wheels of Steel: Pedal to the Metal * Burnout Paradise * Bus Driver * Dirt * Dirt 2 * Driver: San Francisco * Euro Truck Simulator * Euro Truck Simulator 2 * F1 2010 * F1 2011 * F1 2012 * German Truck Simulator * Grand Prix Legends * Grand Theft Auto IV * Grid * GTR 2 – FIA GT Racing Game * GTR Evolution * iRacing.com * Live for Speed * NASCAR The Game: Inside Line * Need for Speed: Most Wanted * Need for Speed: Carbon * Need for Speed: ProStreet * Need for Speed: Shift * Need for Speed: Undercover * Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit * Need for Speed: The Run * OMSI Bus Simulator * rFactor * rFactor 2 * Scania Truck Driving Simulator * Sega Rally Revo * Shift 2: Unleashed * Simraceway * Sonic & Sega All-Stars Racing * World Racing 2 * Test Drive Unlimited * Test Drive Unlimited 2 * TrackMania Nations Forever * Trucks & Trailers * UK Truck Simulator * WRC: FIA World Rally Championship (2010) * WRC 2 FIA World Rally Championship (2011)

page.title: Accessories/Logitech Driving Force Pro Force Feedback Wheel

revision.comment: Point cover at a rendition that actually serves
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Logitech Driving Force Pro Force Feedback Wheel | Guid=3000-142 | Deck=The Logitech Driving Force Pro Force Feedback Wheel for the PlayStation 2 was released in 2004 to coincide with the release of Gran Turismo 4. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395948-5317989433-ACqFvF3KiyQaBOk5oiB4QF_UKMSXrb08msIvDpUH1Ygf0vnruYdRKtZFrXXWEanyqntEZdyoRjiGglg%3Ds1200.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395948-5317989433-ACqFvF3KiyQaBOk5oiB4QF_UKMSXrb08msIvDpUH1Ygf0vnruYdRKtZFrXXWEanyqntEZdyoRjiGglg=s1200.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,square_small,scale_super,scale_small,scale_medium,square_mini,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Logitech Driving Force Pro Force Feedback Wheel for the PlayStation 2 was released in 2004 to coincide with the release of Gran Turismo 4.

{{Accessory | Name=Logitech G27 Racing Wheel | Guid=3000-65 | Deck=A racing wheel from Logitech, compatible with PS2, PS3, and PC. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/5/59593/2605866-2605864-2257886805-25720.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2605866-2605864-2257886805-25720.png","path":"5\/59593\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"square_tiny,square_medium,scale_small,square_small,square_mini,scale_tiny,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' />
==Overview== The Logitech G27 Racing Wheel is compatible with [[Platforms/PlayStation_2|PlayStation 2]], [[Platforms/PlayStation_3|PlayStation 3]], and [[Platforms/PC|PCs]] running Windows XP or later.
==Features== * Force feedback * Gas, brake and clutch pedals * H-pattern shifter * Helical gearing * Paddle shifters * RPM/shift indicator LEDs * 900-degree steering
==System Requirements==
=====Windows 8, Windows 7, Windows Vista, or Windows XP===== * 20 MB of available hard disk space * USB port * Internet connection for software download
==Game Compatibility==
====PS3==== * Burnout Paradise * Dirt * Dirt 2 * Dirt 3 * F1 2010 * F1 2011 * F1 2012 * Ferrari Challenge Trofeo Pirelli * Formula One Championship Edition * Grand Theft Auto IV * Gran Turismo HD Concept * Gran Turismo 5 Prologue 1 2 * Gran Turismo 5 1 2 * Gran Turismo 6 * Initial D Extreme Stage * Juiced 2: Hot Import Nights * Midnight Club: Los Angeles * MotorStorm: Apocalypse * NASCAR 08 * NASCAR 09 * NASCAR The Game: 2011 * Need for Speed: Carbon * Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit * Need for Speed: ProStreet * Need for Speed: The Run * Need for Speed: Shift * Need for Speed: Undercover * Need for Speed: Most Wanted 2012 * GRID * Sega Rally Revo * Shift 2: Unleashed 2 * Sonic & Sega All-Stars Racing * Stuntman: Ignition * Superstars V8 Racing * Supercar Challenge * Test Drive Unlimited 2 * WRC: FIA World Rally Championship 2010 * WRC 3: FIA World Rally Championship 2012
====PS2==== * 007: Nightfire * 18 Wheeler: American Pro Trucker * 4x4 Evolution * Auto Modellista * Burnout * Burnout 2: Point of Impact * Burnout 3: Takedown * Burnout Revenge * Burnout Dominator * Colin McRae Rally 3 * Colin McRae Rally 04 * Colin McRae Rally 2005 * Corvette * Corvette Evolution GT * Driven * EA Sports F1 2001 * EA Sports F1 2002 * EA Sports F1 Career Challenge * Enthusia Professional Racing * Ferrari Challenge: Trofeo Pirelli * Ferrari F355 Challenge * FlatOut * FlatOut 2 * Ford Mustang: The Legend Lives * Ford vs. Chevy * Formula One 2001 * Formula One 2002 * Formula One 2003 * Formula One 04 * Formula One 05 * Formula One 06 * Gran Turismo 3: A-Spec * Gran Turismo 4 * Gran Turismo 4 Prologue * Gran Turismo Concept * Grand Prix Challenge * Grand Theft Auto III * Grand Theft Auto: Vice City * Hot Wheels Velocity X * Hot Wheels World Race * Hot Wheels: Stunt Track Challenge * IndyCar Series * IndyCar Series 2005 * Initial D: Special Stage * Juiced (video game) * Juiced 2: Hot Import Nights * Jurassic Park: Operation Genesis * Knight Rider: The Game * Knight Rider 2: The Game * Lotus Challenge * Midnight Club II * Midnight Club 3: DUB Edition * Motor Mayhem * NASCAR 06: Total Team Control * NASCAR 07 * NASCAR 08 * NASCAR 09 * NASCAR: Dirt to Daytona * NASCAR Heat 2002 * NASCAR Thunder 2002 * NASCAR Thunder 2003 * NASCAR Thunder 2004 * NASCAR 2005: Chase for the Cup * Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit 2 * Need for Speed: Underground * Need for Speed: Underground 2 * Need for Speed: Most Wanted * Need for Speed: Carbon * Need for Speed: ProStreet * Need for Speed: Undercover * NHRA Championship Drag Racing * R: Racing Evolution * Rally Championship * Rally Fusion: Race of Champions * Richard Burns Rally * RoadKill * Sega Rally 2006 * Shox * Smuggler's Run 2: Hostile Territory * Starsky & Hutch * The Simpsons: Hit & Run * The Simpsons: Road Rage * Total Immersion Racing * Test Drive Overdrive * Test Drive: Eve of Destruction * TOCA Race Driver 2 * TOCA Race Driver 3 * Tokyo Xtreme Racer 3 * Tokyo Xtreme Racer: Drift 2 * Total Immersion Racing * Twisted Metal: Black * Twisted Metal: Head-On * V-Rally 3 * World of Outlaws: Sprint Cars 2002 * World Rally Championship * WRC II Extreme * WRC 3 * WRC 4 * WRC: Rally Evolved
====PC==== * 18 Wheels of Steel: Across America * 18 Wheels of Steel: American Long Haul * 18 Wheels of Steel: Convoy * 18 Wheels of Steel: Extreme Trucker * 18 Wheels of Steel: Extreme Trucker 2 * 18 Wheels of Steel: Hard Truck * 18 Wheels of Steel: Haulin' * 18 Wheels of Steel: Pedal to the Metal * Burnout Paradise * Bus Driver * Dirt * Dirt 2 * Driver: San Francisco * Euro Truck Simulator * Euro Truck Simulator 2 * F1 2010 * F1 2011 * F1 2012 * German Truck Simulator * Grand Prix Legends * Grand Theft Auto IV * Grid * GTR 2 – FIA GT Racing Game * GTR Evolution * iRacing.com * Live for Speed * NASCAR The Game: Inside Line * Need for Speed: Most Wanted * Need for Speed: Carbon * Need for Speed: ProStreet * Need for Speed: Shift * Need for Speed: Undercover * Need for Speed: Hot Pursuit * Need for Speed: The Run * OMSI Bus Simulator * rFactor * rFactor 2 * Scania Truck Driving Simulator * Sega Rally Revo * Shift 2: Unleashed * Simraceway * Sonic & Sega All-Stars Racing * World Racing 2 * Test Drive Unlimited * Test Drive Unlimited 2 * TrackMania Nations Forever * Trucks & Trailers * UK Truck Simulator * WRC: FIA World Rally Championship (2010) * WRC 2 FIA World Rally Championship (2011)

{{Accessory | Name=Mega Modem | Guid=3000-114 | Deck=An early Japanese-exclusive dial-up modem accessory for the Sega Mega Drive. }}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":[],"background":[]}' />
==Overview== The Mega Modem is a first-party dial-up modem accessory developed by [[Companies/Sega|Sega]] for the [[Platforms/Genesis|Sega Mega Drive]] exclusively in Japan on November 3, 1990. The peripheral was almost sold in North America as the ''TeleGenesis'' modem, but this plan was scrapped prior to release. One of the few (only?) peripherals to support the 9-pin EXT port of the first-generation Sega Mega Drive, the Mega Modem allowed certain games and software to make dial-up connections through an attached telephone line. The peripheral itself also includes a built-in microphone and speaker for use with some software. As the modem was limited to slow 1.2 kbps speed connections, only a handful of games use the peripheral for turn-based communication. In addition, the peripheral was used for other services (all requiring a monthly subscription fee): * Digital game distribution through the [[Concepts/Sega_Meganet|Sega Game Toshokan / Sega Game Library]] software cartridge. With a 800円 monthly fee, players could download small-sized games to the cartridge for play, released between 1990 and 1992. Most of these games were exclusive to the service at the time, with most released later as part of the [[Franchises/Game_no_Kanzume|Game no Kanzume]] series and some official emulation services. It also included game soundtracks and an online newsletter. * Digital home banking using the ''Mega Anser'' software cartridge and its bundled Ten Key Pad input peripheral (and optional thermal printer attachment). Specialized versions of the cartridge were made for specific financial institutions: ''Naisu-kun Mini'', ''Osaka Ginkou no Home Banking Service: My Line'', and ''Sumisei Home Tanmatsu''. * Receiving game statistics, both real-time and post-game, for professional baseball through the ''Nikkan Sports Pro Yakyuu VAN'' software cartridge. This utilized a monthly subscription fee and can show a visualized representation of each play as if it was a video game. These concepts were later revisited for the Mega Drive using cartridge-based modems, including the [[Concepts/Sega_Channel|Sega Channel]] (an official digital game distribution service using coaxial cable connections), the Mega Net (an online multi-use service for Brazil from [[Companies/Tec_Toy_Industria_de_Brinquedos_S_A|Tec Toy]], used for e-mail, banking, and chat), and the XBAND (an online multiplayer service from Catapult Entertainment).
==Supported Games== Only seven retail games supported the Mega Modem peripheral: * [[Games/Advanced_Daisenryaku_Deutsch_Dengeki_Sakusen|Advanced Daisenryaku: Deutsch Dengeki Sakusen]] * [[Games/Cyberball|Cyberball]] * [[Games/Go_Net|GO-NET]] * [[Games/Shi_Kin_Joh|Shikinjou]] * San San: Igo Tsuushin Taikyoku Network * [[Games/Tel_Tel_Mahjong|Tel-Tel Mahjong]] * [[Games/Tel_Tel_Stadium|Tel-Tel Stadium]] As the modem was limited to 1.2 kbps speeds, most games use the peripheral for turn-based multiplayer. Shikinjou uses the service instead for distributing custom stages built with the [[Concepts/In_Game_Map_Editor|built-in editor]]. Both San San and GO-NET used the peripheral to connect to their own online service, both of which were later discontinued. Both were also the only play-by-mail games for the system, allowing players to continue their games at any time (even picking up the game on their respective [[Platforms/PC|PC]] clients).

{{Accessory | Name=Memory Stick Duo | Guid=3000-155 | Deck=Designed by Sony for use many of their electronics, the Memory Stick Duo (and Pro Duo) is the official storage device for the PlayStation Portable. Sizes compatible with the PSP range from 32 MB to 128 GB. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396240-8638329405-d19f4b.jpeg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396240-8638329405-d19f4b.jpeg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_medium,scale_small,scale_super,square_mini,square_medium,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium,square_tiny","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Designed by Sony for use many of their electronics, the Memory Stick Duo (and Pro Duo) is the official storage device for the PlayStation Portable. Sizes compatible with the PSP range from 32 MB to 128 GB.

{{Accessory | Name=Microsoft HoloLens | Guid=3000-86 | Deck=HoloLens is a wireless set of augmented reality goggles developed by Microsoft. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/14/141373/2762091-8911123746-micro.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2762091-8911123746-micro.jpg","path":"14\/141373\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_tiny,square_medium,square_small,square_mini,scale_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' />
==Overview== Microsoft Hololens is a mixed reality head mounted display that runs on a new computing platform called Windows Holographic.
==Technology== Built into Hololens is a depth camera with a 120°×120° angle of view, a microphone array, and a photographic video camera. The unit also includes an accelerometer, gyroscope, and a magnetometer.
The unit also has built in stereo speakers that do not block the users ability to hear the real world environment.
==Games Supported== Currently, the only game that has been officially announced for Hololens is [[Games/Minecraft|Minecraft]]. At [[Concepts/E3_2015|E3 2015]], Microsoft showed off a prototype demo off first person shooter called Project X-ray.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-59)

revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=NES Controller | Guid=3000-119 | Deck=The official controller for the Nintendo Entertainment System. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395486-4615400797-nesct.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395486-4615400797-nesct.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_medium,square_medium,scale_super,square_small,scale_small,square_tiny,screen_kubrick,scale_large,square_mini,scale_avatar,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official controller for the Nintendo Entertainment System.

{{Accessory | Name=NES Max | Guid=3000-49 | Deck=First party NES controller featuring turbo buttons and an 8-way sliding directional input called a cycloid. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/12/120008/2458964-5816969847-NES-m.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2458964-5816969847-NES-m.jpg","path":"12\/120008\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,square_mini,scale_small,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,scale_tiny,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> Released by Nintendo in 1988, the Max is distinguished by its unique sliding "cycloid" control disk. The cycloid replaces the standard NES D-pad, allowing for 8-way control. Around the cycloid is a black plastic ring that can be used for more traditional directional control. Below the B & A buttons are two gray, dome shaped turbo buttons. The controller has something of a wing shape, similar to the PlayStation controller, only thinner and flatter.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-20)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=NES Power Pad | Guid=3000-20 | Deck=An NES peripheral that allows games to be controlled by hitting pressure sensors inside a mat, ideally with one's feet. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/5/59593/2604997-powerpad_cropped.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2604997-powerpad_cropped.jpg","path":"5\/59593\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_tiny,square_medium,scale_small,square_small,square_mini,scale_tiny,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' />
==Overview==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_medium/0/25/10953-Wallpaper_TRU_001.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/25/10953-Wallpaper_TRU_001.jpg|align=right|width=480|caption=A family drifts through space while enjoying Bandai's original version of the device}} The Power Pad is a controller for the [[Platforms/Nintendo_Entertainment_System|Nintendo Entertainment System]], consisting of a flexible mat with pressure sensors that function as buttons. It was first released by [[Companies/Bandai_Co_Ltd|Bandai]] in [[Locations/Japan|Japan]] in 1986 under the name Family Trainer. In 1988 it was released in [[Locations/North_America|North America]] as Family Fun Fitness. It became the Power Pad when Nintendo bought the rights that same year, and re-released it alongside [[Games/Stadium_Events|World Class Track Meet]]. Although other similar step controllers existed (notably, the '[[Games/Video_Jogger|Foot Craz]]' for the Atari 2600), the Power Pad was the first commercially successful controller of this type.
The pad is double-sided, with side A having eight buttons and side B having twelve buttons. Power Pad games primarily test the player's speed, timing, coordination, and memory, though it also enabled music-focused gameplay that would serve as a precursor to rhythm games such as [[Franchises/Dance_Dance_Revolution|Dance Dance Revolution]].
[[Companies/Bandai_Namco_Entertainment|Namco Bandai]] later released a Power Pad-like device for the [[Platforms/Wii|Wii]] in 2008 called the Active Life, which worked in conjunction with the [[Accessories/Wii_Remote|Wii Remote]].
==Power Pad Games==
{{GBFigure|src=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_small/0/25/10963-AthleticWorld.jpg|full=http://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/0/25/10963-AthleticWorld.jpg|align=left|width=320|caption=Athletic World - 1986}} * [[Games/Athletic_World|Athletic World]] * [[Games/Dance_Aerobics|Dance Aerobics]] * [[Games/Family_Trainer_Series_4_Jogging_Race|Jogging Race]] * Famitre Daiundokai * Fuun! Takeshi Jo Ni * Meiro Daisakusen * Manhattan Police * Rai Rai! Kyonshis * Running Stadium * [[Games/Short_Order_Eggsplode|Short Order/Eggsplode!]] * [[Games/Street_Cop|Street Cop]] * Super Team Games * Totsugeki! Fuun Takeshi Jo * World Class Track Meet ([[Games/Stadium_Events|Stadium Events]])

{{Accessory | Name=NES Speed Board | Guid=3000-79 | Deck=An NES accessory that was designed to hold a controller so the user didn't have to. Released in 1991, the idea was that it would allow the player to press buttons faster. It was poorly received and production was halted shortly after its release. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/12/120008/2697784-7534798549-speed.jpg}}

{{Accessory | Name=NES Zapper | Guid=3000-120 | Deck=The official light gun accessory for the Nintendo Entertainment System. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395488-9010280350-nes-z.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395488-9010280350-nes-z.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_super,scale_small,square_medium,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official light gun accessory for the Nintendo Entertainment System.

{{Accessory | Name=Namco GunCon 2 Light Gun | Guid=3000-143 | Deck=Namco's officially licensed light gun for the PlayStation 2. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395951-8410181747-812of.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395951-8410181747-812of.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,square_medium,scale_small,scale_medium,square_mini,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Namco's officially licensed light gun for the PlayStation 2.

{{Accessory | Name=Net Yaroze Controller | Guid=3000-128 | Deck=The official controller designed by Sony Computer Entertainment for use with the Net Yaroze, which is a commercially sold development kit version of the original PlayStation. Model number DTL-H3010, it is charcoal gray in color, which matches the Net Yaroze console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395548-0827048797-Plays.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395548-0827048797-Plays.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_medium,scale_super,square_small,scale_tiny,scale_small,scale_medium,square_mini,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_medium,square_avatar,square_tiny","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official controller designed by Sony Computer Entertainment for use with the Net Yaroze, which is a commercially sold development kit version of the original PlayStation. Model number DTL-H3010, it is charcoal gray in color, which matches the Net Yaroze console.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo 64 Controller | Guid=3000-131 | Deck=The official controller designed by Nintendo for use with the Nintendo 64. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395563-9953786443-N64-C.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395563-9953786443-N64-C.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,scale_medium,scale_super,scale_tiny,scale_small,square_mini,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official controller designed by Nintendo for use with the Nintendo 64.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo 64 Controller Pak | Guid=3000-132 | Deck=The official storage device designed by Nintendo for use with the Nintendo 64. The original model (NUS-004) holds 32KB of data. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395631-2106517398-il_fu.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395631-2106517398-il_fu.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,scale_small,scale_medium,square_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The official storage device designed by Nintendo for use with the Nintendo 64. The original model (NUS-004) holds 32KB of data.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo 64 Expansion Pak | Guid=3000-134 | Deck=The Expansion Pak adds 4MB of RAM to the Nintendo 64 console. Originally designed for the 64DD add-on, it is still required for several standard N64 games. In North America, it was packaged with Donkey Kong 64. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395640-3190802743-latest.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395640-3190802743-latest.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,scale_medium,scale_small,square_mini,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,scale_large,square_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Expansion Pak adds 4MB of RAM to the Nintendo 64 console. Originally designed for the 64DD add-on, it is still required for several standard N64 games. In North America, it was packaged with Donkey Kong 64.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo 64 Jumper Pak | Guid=3000-133 | Deck=The Jumper Pak comes pre-installed in every Nintendo 64 and essentially is a placeholder for the eventual release of the Expansion Pak, which provides more RAM for the console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395635-4263610634-Ninte.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395635-4263610634-Ninte.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,scale_small,square_small,scale_medium,square_medium,square_mini,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,screen_medium,square_avatar,square_tiny","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Jumper Pak comes pre-installed in every Nintendo 64 and essentially is a placeholder for the eventual release of the Expansion Pak, which provides more RAM for the console.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo 64 Rumble Pak | Guid=3000-135 | Deck=The Rumble Pak plugs into the back of the Nintendo 64 controller and provides haptic feedback for many of the console's games. It is powered by two AAA batteries. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395645-screenshot2022-07-31at8.00.39pm.png}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395645-screenshot2022-07-31at8.00.39pm.png","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/png","sizes":"scale_super,square_small,square_medium,scale_small,scale_medium,square_tiny,square_mini,scale_avatar,screen_kubrick,scale_large,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> The Rumble Pak plugs into the back of the Nintendo 64 controller and provides haptic feedback for many of the console's games. It is powered by two AAA batteries.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo Switch 2 Camera | Guid=3000-203 | Deck=A USB camera for use with Nintendo Switch 2's Game Chat feature. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3717042-6017384614-Frame_3473725.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3717042-6017384614-Frame_3473725.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,scale_medium,square_small,square_medium,scale_tiny,scale_small,square_mini,square_tiny,scale_large,scale_avatar,square_avatar,screen_medium,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A USB camera for use with Nintendo Switch 2's Game Chat feature.

{{Accessory | Name=Nintendo Switch 2 Pro Controller | Guid=3000-199 | Deck=The Nintendo Switch 2 Pro Controller includes the console's new capture button as well as exclusive GL and GR buttons on the back of the grips. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3717024-4966751396-Frame_3473717.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3717024-4966751396-Frame_3473717.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_medium,square_small,scale_small,scale_medium,square_tiny,scale_avatar,square_mini,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> The Nintendo Switch 2 Pro Controller includes the console's new capture button as well as exclusive GL and GR buttons on the back of the grips.

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-184)

revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=OUYA Controller | Guid=3000-72 | Deck=The primary input device for the OUYA video game console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/16/164924/2622150-0300322487-zshds.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2622150-0300322487-zshds.jpg","path":"16\/164924\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_small,square_tiny,square_medium,scale_tiny,square_mini,square_avatar,scale_medium,screen_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,scale_super,ignore_jpg_scale_super,screen_kubrick","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> The OUYA controller has a number of features: * Two thumbsticks * Raised cross D-Pad * Four face buttons (O, U, Y, and A) * Center touchpad * Four triggers: two left, and two right * Ⓤ Menu/Pause button * Wireless Bluetooth connectivity * Swappable faceplates

revision.comment: Restore image alignment, sizes and captions from legacy source (3000-41)
revision.text:
{{Accessory | Name=Oculus Rift | Guid=3000-41 | Deck=The Oculus Rift is a virtual reality headset for the PC released in March 2016. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/1/10354/2839252-logo1blog.jpg}}
==Overview== Oculus Rift is a virtual reality headset developed by [[Companies/Oculus_Studios|Oculus VR]], headed by Palmer Luckey. The headset's primary features are low-latency head-tracking and a wide field of view. The Rift is being built with out-of-the-box engine integration with [https://www.giantbomb.com/unreal-engine-3/3015-86 Unreal Engine] and [https://www.giantbomb.com/unity-engine/3015-5683 Unity]. The consumer version of the Rift was released in March 2016 and pre-orders began on 6th January. A wireless Xbox One controller will come packed in with every consumer version of the device along with [[Games/EVE_Valkyrie|EVE: Valkyrie]], and [[Games/Luckys_Tale|Lucky's Tale]]. ==Kickstarter== In August 2012, Oculus VR launched a [https://www.giantbomb.com/kickstarter-funded/3015-7378 Kickstarter] campaign in order to fund the production of developer prototypes of the Rift, with a goal of $250,000. Rewards included t-shirts, posters and, for those pledging $300 or more, the development prototype and access to the SDK after the first production run. The kickstarter goal was met, with the total amount pledged almost reaching $2,5 million by roughly 9,500 backers. After the Kickstarter, Oculus VR opened up pre-orders for the same prototype at the same price on their website. Production of the Rift prototype began, with shipping initially estimated to begin in December 2012. ==Facebook Acquisition== On March 25th 2014, Facebook announced that they would be [https://www.giantbomb.com/articles/facebook-just-bought-oculus-vr/1100-4883 acquiring Oculus VR] for $2 billion in cash and shares of Facebook stock.
“Mobile is the platform of today, and now we’re also getting ready for the platforms of tomorrow. Oculus has the chance to create the most social platform ever, and change the way we work, play and communicate.” [https://newsroom.fb.com/news/2014/03/facebook-to-acquire-oculus Facebook founder and CEO, Mark Zuckerberg.]
After the announcement, Minecraft creator [[People/Markus_Persson|Notch]] announced on his [http://notch.net/2014/03/virtual-reality-is-going-to-change-the-world personal blog] that he would be cancelling talks with Oculus VR regarding working with them in order to bring Minecraft to the rift. https://twitter.com/notch/status/448586381565390848 ==Prototype== The initial features of the Rift protoype, as detailed on the Kickstarter page, were: * 6 degrees of freedom motion sensors * 1280x800 resolution * Fish-eye optics * A high field of view(90 degrees horizontal, 110 degrees diagonal) * Packed in of [[Games/Doom_3|Doom 3 BFG Edition]], the first game to support the Rift Notable differences between the Rift and other head-mounted displays were the low latency motion sensors and the fish-eye optics. A 6DOF motion sensor includes two 3-axis sensors, an accelerometer for tracking orientation and a magnetometer for reducing error in pitch and roll. The fish-eye lenses were meant to focus the user’s vision on the display in order to minimize the effect of looking at a screen at a distance, which other HMD’s suffer from. ===Prototype revision=== A Kickstarter update sent out in late 2012 revealed that, because of the unexpectedly high amount of prototypes needing production, the production pipeline had to be changed. This resulted in a delay, with shipping estimated to occur in March 2013, as well as some improvements to the hardware of the prototype. The screen used, while the same resolution, would be larger(7” instead of the original 5.6”), with faster pixel switching as well as having a higher contrast and pixel fill factor. Having a higher fill factor means having the pixels take up a larger portion of the panel, reducing the visible black grid seen between pixels(the “screen door effect”). A large addition was the change from 6DOF sensors to 9DOF sensors, meaning the Rift would additionally incorporate a 3-axis magnetometer. ==Development Kit 2== The second version of developer hardware began shipping out to those who pre-ordered them for $350 starting early July 2014. {{GBFigure|src=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/ignore_jpg_scale_medium/3/38925/2665204-2128746083-camer.jpg|full=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/original/3/38925/2665204-2128746083-camer.jpg|align=left|width=480}} While not identical to the planned consumer Rift, key additions are present which include a higher resolution screen with a low-persistence screen and a sensor that allows low-latency positional head tracking. * 960 x 1080 resolution per eye (1920 x 1080 screen) * 75 Hz, 72 Hz, 60 Hz Refresh Rate * Positional Tracking using Near Infrared CMOS Sensor at 60 Hz * 100 degree Field of View * Gyroscope, Accelerometer, Magnetometer sensors updating at 1000 Hz * 0.97 lbs (without cables) ==Supported games== The Oculus Rift currently supports the following game releases: * [[Games/Doom_3|Doom 3 BFG Edition]] * [[Games/Hawken|Hawken]] * [[Games/Team_Fortress_2|Team Fortress 2]] * [https://www.giantbomb.com/half-life-2/3030-1539 Half Life 2] * [[Games/Slender_The_Arrival|Slender: The Arrival]] ==Trivia== * In 1991, [[Companies/Sega|Sega]] introduced a similar [[Concepts/Head_Tracking|head-tracking]], [[Concepts/Virtual_Reality|VR]] headset called the [[Accessories/Sega_VR|Sega VR]]. After various demonstrations at shows and [[Platforms/Arcade|arcades]] in the 90's (such as the [[Games/Sega_VR_1|Sega VR-1]] attraction), the device was not heard from again. Oculus VR mentioned the Sega headset as one of the influences that they wanted to improve on.

{{Accessory | Name=Oculus Touch | Guid=3000-85 | Deck=Oculus Touch is the name of Oculus' motion-tracking controllers for use with its Rift VR headset. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/14/141373/2762108-2661997474-oculu.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"2762108-2661997474-oculu.jpg","path":"14\/141373\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_tiny,square_medium,square_mini,square_small,scale_small,square_avatar,screen_medium,scale_medium,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_large,screen_kubrick,screen_small,screen_tiny","caption":""},"background":[]}' /> The Oculus Touch controllers were unveiled as part of the company's pre-E3 press conference for 2015. The controllers are specifically referred to as the "Half Moon Prototype."

{{Accessory | Name=PDP Mortal Kombat Fight Stick | Guid=3000-167 | Deck=PDP's Mortal Kombat Fight Stick was released in conjunction with Mortal Kombat (2011) and features the classic X-shaped button layout from the MK arcade games. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396572-9171928352-b82c8.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396572-9171928352-b82c8.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_small,scale_medium,scale_super,square_tiny,square_medium,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_medium,screen_kubrick,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> PDP's Mortal Kombat Fight Stick was released in conjunction with Mortal Kombat (2011) and features the classic X-shaped button layout from the MK arcade games.

{{Accessory | Name=PSone LCD Screen | Guid=3000-130 | Deck=A 5-inch LCD screen designed by Sony Computer Entertainment for the redesigned PlayStation (PSone) in September 2000. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3395554-6953548755-s-l64.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3395554-6953548755-s-l64.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"square_small,square_medium,square_tiny,scale_super,scale_tiny,scale_small,scale_medium,square_mini,screen_kubrick,scale_large,scale_avatar,screen_medium,square_avatar","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> A 5-inch LCD screen designed by Sony Computer Entertainment for the redesigned PlayStation (PSone) in September 2000.

{{Accessory | Name=PlayStation 2 40GB Hard Drive | Guid=3000-144 | Deck=Released in July 2001 in Japan and March 2004 in North America, the PlayStation 2 Hard Disk Drive is used to enhance certain games' features such as load times, instant replays, and increased storage. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396001-0501619312-2200p.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396001-0501619312-2200p.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_super,square_medium,square_tiny,square_small,scale_small,scale_medium,square_mini,scale_avatar,scale_large,screen_kubrick,square_avatar,screen_medium","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Released in July 2001 in Japan and March 2004 in North America, the PlayStation 2 Hard Disk Drive is used to enhance certain games' features such as load times, instant replays, and increased storage.

{{Accessory | Name=PlayStation 2 Horizontal Stand | Guid=3000-146 | Deck=Quite possibly the most useless accessory of all time, the Horizontal Stand released by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation 2 simply adds a blue accent to the front of the console. | Image=https://www.giantbomb.com/a/uploads/scale_super/0/1992/3396033-8725363064-ps2-s.jpg}}
<div id='imageData' data-json='{"infobox":{"file":"3396033-8725363064-ps2-s.jpg","path":"0\/1992\/","mime":"image\/jpeg","sizes":"scale_small,square_medium,scale_super,scale_medium,square_small,square_tiny,square_avatar,screen_medium,scale_avatar,square_mini,scale_large,screen_kubrick","caption":null},"background":[]}' /> Quite possibly the most useless accessory of all time, the Horizontal Stand released by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation 2 simply adds a blue accent to the front of the console.
